supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XXIV NO. 5, MARCH 16 1912
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
Home Rule.—Irish Incident Showing Feeling Towards

Britain.—By One Who Has Lived in Ireland,” must

cost the * Dundee Courier "’ £1,500. It may be more

canny next time.

General libels are cheap. The ““ Irish Times’’ had

an infamous libel on the Nationalists and Catholics of

Dundalk a few days ago in the form of an anonymous

letter.  That libel was denounced in the local paper,

the “ Dundalk Democrat,”’ over his own name by a

Scotch. non-Catholic settled in Dundalk, as a conse-

quence of our Dundalk contemporary quoting our notes

about it.  But up to this day we have not seen any

protest against it in the *‘ Irish Times,”’ nor have we

seen any apology in the ‘‘ Irish Times ' for its gross

libel.  The libel was so general that it might be im-

contradictory_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XXIV NO. 8, APRIL 6 1912
contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
flung info it from the other side of the Channel. Day .

by day the evil grew, and the Irish youth, whose minds

were thus distorted, seized more and more eagerly on

this gutter Press. =~ We have but to turn back the

pages of our Liistory, we have but to enter into conversa-

tion with some veteran of the West to realize something

of the old native culture. ~Where the English literary

refuse has not come to destroy and where the traditions

still remain, there is in the most unlettered: person a

distinctive intellectual quality.  Side by side, there-

fore, they can be placed: the Irishman of the older

generation, possessing his remnant of Gaelic culture

and who, perhaps, has never opened any book, and the

young man whose literary education has been formed

smmense  demonstrations were written by him for the *‘Cork
Examiner,”’ and afterwards copied into the Dublin and English press.

0'Neill Daunt in his ““Ireland and her Agitators,” the last edition of

)

which appeared in 1888 as ‘85 years of Irish History,” pays a fine
sribute to Lenihan for his services as a political organiser and journalist.
The ‘‘Liberator”’ warmly admired him, and when. Lenihan founded the
« Tipperary Vindicator” at Nenagh in 1844, O’Connell wrote him a
very appreciative Tetter and for half a century this letter was on view
in his office in Limerick, to which city he removed in 1849 on taking

1)

over the ‘‘ Limerick Reporter,” a journal he had previously edited for
two years, 1841-43, when he accepted an engagement from John Francis
Maguire on the staff of tho ‘‘ Cork Examiner.”’ It was during Lenihan’s
editorship of the  Tipperary Vindicator ”’ that he exposed the famous
police plot known as ‘‘ The Shinrone Conspiracy,” and obtained the
dismissal of a large number of constables with their leader.

To ono of his marked hiterary gifts and antiquarian ta,q‘tes Limerick
